[1] Pemphigus Vulgaris - Medscape Reference
Pemphigus vulgaris is a chronic autoimmune blistering disease primarily affecting adults aged 40–60 years. It causes flaking and painless blisters on the skin and oral mucosa leading to ulceration and discomfort. The primary trigger is autoantibodies against desmoglein 3 (Dsg3), resulting in loss of adhesion between epithelial cells. The disease course is variable depending on severity and immune status. Treatment typically begins with systemic corticosteroids administered orally or intravenously along with immunosuppressive agents such as azathioprine or methotrexate. For refractory cases, rituximab may be added later in therapy. Prognosis depends largely on timely initiation of treatment and control of flare-ups through long-term maintenance regimens. Complications include infection risks due to open wounds and nutritional deficiencies secondary to mouth ulcers.
[2] Pemphigus Vulgaris - Mayo Clinic
Similar to Mespugus vulgaris (likely typo), pemphigus vulgaris affects mainly middle-aged adults and leads to severe itching and burning sensations before developing blisters and erosive lesions on exposed surfaces such as hands, feet, arms, legs, scalp, and genitalia. Oral manifestations are very common and lead to difficulty eating and talking. The disease progresses slowly over months until full-blown eruption develops. Patients experience recurrent episodes triggered by stressors or infections. Management starts with high-dose steroids combined with other medications aimed at reducing overall immune activation levels.
[3] Pemphigus Vulgaris - UpToDate
Pemphigus vulgaris arises from IgG-mediated destruction of desmosomes containing desmoglein proteins located at intercellular junctions in suprabasal layers of epidermis and mucosal epithelia. Autoimmune attack targets predominantly desmoglein 3 (Dsg3) in most cases (approximately 80%-90%). Loss of cohesion between adjacent keratinocytes leads to acantholysis—an event responsible for intraepidermal blister formation. Clinical presentation includes tense blisters prone to rupture easily producing erosive surface lesions filled with serum-rich fluid. Common sites involve buccal mucosa, scalp, groin region, axillae, back, chest wall—all areas subject to friction or exposure. Early recognition allows better outcome prediction and improved quality-of-life measures through aggressive therapeutic interventions targeting humoral immunity effectively.
[4] Pemphigus Vulgaris - Wikipedia
Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune disease wherein antibodies target desmoglein 3 protein expressed mostly in skin and oral mucosa tissues. Its incidence increases slightly with advancing age starting around midlife (~40 years old onward). It predominantly impacts males who are otherwise healthy but may progress rapidly if untreated or poorly managed initially.
[5] Pemphigus Vulgaris - PubMed Central
Autoimmunity plays a key role in triggering pemphigus vulgaris via production of anti-desmoglein antibodies directed against epitopes shared across many desmoglein isoforms (especially Dsg3). This results in disruption of desmosome integrity leading ultimately to separation of keratinocyte sheets forming superficial blisters that break down quickly into shallow ulcers rich in serous exudates.
[6] Pemphigus Vulgaris - National Institute of Health
Pemphigus vulgaris belongs to a group of rare autoimmune disorders collectively referred to as pemphigids. These diseases share similar histopathological features involving breakdown of intercellular connections mediated by antibody attacks on components inside desmosomes—particularly desmogleins—and thus lead to widespread blistering across skin and mucus membranes.

There is no known genetic predisposition involved in pemphigus vulgaris development; instead, environmental triggers such as drugs or infections initiate spontaneous autoimmune responses in susceptible hosts. While family history may occasionally report clusters suggesting possible susceptibility factors yet no formal Mendelian model has ever been proposed for this condition.

One study explored potential links between HLA alleles and susceptibility to pemphigus vulgaris but found inconsistent correlations across populations—no conclusive evidence supports a monogenic or autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance for this illness.

Several case reports highlight instances where siblings developed simultaneous outbreaks—raising concern about shared environment versus coincidental timing—but none indicate inherited transmission patterns nor familial aggregation beyond chance occurrences.
[10] Pemphigus Vulgaris - Epidemiology
Epidemiologically, pemphigus vulgaris shows low prevalence rates globally ranging from <1/100,000 people annually worldwide—with higher incidences noted among older adult populations living in industrialized nations.

None of the sources state that pemphigus vulgaris has autosomal dominant inheritance. There is no mention of Mendelian inheritance models or familial patterns tied to specific genes passed down generations. Some papers note weak associations with HLA markers but nothing approaching autosomal dominance.

Pemphigus vulgaris is a chronic autoimmune blistering disease primarily affecting adults aged 40–60 years. Oral manifestations are very common and lead to difficulty eating and talking. The disease progresses slowly over months until full-blown eruption develops. Patients experience recurrent episodes triggered by stressors or infections. Management starts with high-dose steroids combined with other medications aimed at reducing overall immune activation levels.
[3] Pemphigus Vulgaris - UpToDate
Pemphigus vulgaris arises from IgG-mediated destruction of desmosomes containing desmoglein proteins located at intercellular junctions in suprabasal layers of epidermis and mucosal epithelia. Autoimmune attack targets predominantly desmoglein 3 (Dsg3) in most cases (approximately 80%-90%). Loss of cohesion between adjacent keratinocytes leads to acantholysis—an event responsible for intraepidermal blister formation. Clinical presentation includes tense blisters prone to rupture easily producing erosive surface lesions filled with serum-rich fluid. Common sites involve buccal mucosa, scalp, groin region, axillae, back, chest wall—all areas subject to friction or exposure. Early recognition allows better outcome prediction and improved quality-of-life measures through aggressive therapeutic interventions targeting humoral immunity effectively.
